events.event: Yellowstone becomes the world's first national park; the concept inspires later British conservation efforts.
The birth of Nik Kershaw, the prominent British musician and songwriter of the 1980s.
Referendums in Scotland and Wales result in a lack of support for devolution at that time.
The death of John Wesley, the English theologian and founder of Methodism.
The birth of Daniel Craig, the actor famous for his portrayal of James Bond.
The Anglo-French supersonic airliner Concorde makes its maiden flight.
The birth of Alexander Graham Bell in Edinburgh, the inventor of the telephone.
The UK miners' strike ends as the National Union of Mineworkers votes to return to work.
The coronation of King Faisal II of Iraq is attended by high-ranking British officials.
The death of Saladin; his chivalry during the Crusades remains a topic of study in British history.
King Charles II grants a charter to William Penn for what would become Pennsylvania.
The birth of Kenny Dalglish, the highly decorated Scotland and Liverpool footballer and manager.
The death of Pierre-Simon Laplace; his mathematical work was vital to British astronomical calculations.
The Supermarine Spitfire makes its first test flight at Eastleigh Aerodrome.
Winston Churchill gives the 'Iron Curtain' speech in Fulton, Missouri.
The Battle of the Alamo ends; the event was widely reported in the British press of the time.
The birth of Dame Kiri Te Kanawa; the New Zealand soprano's career is closely tied to the UK's Royal Opera House.
The MS Herald of Free Enterprise capsizes off Zeebrugge, resulting in 193 deaths.
The birth of Edwin Landseer, the painter known for his depictions of animals and the lions at Trafalgar Square.
The birth of Eduardo Paolozzi, the Scottish artist and sculptor who pioneered pop art.
The birth of Rachel Weisz, the Academy Award-winning British actress.
The death of William III and the accession of Queen Anne to the British throne.
The first meeting of the British Ladies' Football Club is held in London.
The birth of Jordan Pickford, the current England national team goalkeeper.
David Rizzio, the secretary to Mary, Queen of Scots, is murdered at Holyrood Palace.
Adam Smith's 'The Wealth of Nations' is published in London.
The birth of Linda Robson, the popular British actress and television presenter.
The Prince of Wales (later Edward VII) marries Princess Alexandra of Denmark at Windsor.
Crufts Dog Show is held in London for the first time.
The birth of Prince Edward, Earl of Wessex, at Buckingham Palace.
The Indian Mutiny ends; the conflict significantly altered British rule in India.
The birth of Douglas Adams in Cambridge, author of 'The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy'.
The birth of Didier Drogba; the Chelsea player's career is a major part of British football history.
The first mainline electric train in the UK runs from Liverpool to Southport.
The birth of Laszlo L. Berke; the scientist's contributions to aviation were studied by British engineers.
The birth of Liza Minnelli; her performances in London remain legendary in the UK theatre world.
Sir William Herschel discovers the planet Uranus from his garden in Bath.
The birth of Sir Thomas Adams; the philanthropist's work supported many British social initiatives.
The Dunblane school massacre takes place, leading to a major ban on handguns in the UK.
Admiral John Byng is executed on HMS Monarch for 'failing to do his utmost' at the Battle of Minorca.
The birth of Albert Einstein; his theories were famously tested by British astronomer Arthur Eddington.
The death of Karl Marx in London, where he had lived in exile for many years.
The birth of Sir John Snow, the British physician who pioneered epidemiology during the cholera outbreak.
Rolls-Royce Ltd is officially registered in Manchester.
The birth of Craig Revel Horwood, the well-known judge on 'Strictly Come Dancing'.
The first ever FA Cup Final is held at The Oval; Wanderers beat Royal Engineers 1-0.
The birth of Bette Ford; the model and actress was a popular figure in UK fashion media.
The birth of H.P. Baxxter; the musician's work gained a significant following in the UK dance scene.
The British Parliament repeals the Stamp Act following intense protests in the American colonies.
The birth of Alexander McQueen, the visionary British fashion designer.
The birth of Katie Ledecky; the swimmer's Olympic achievements are closely followed by UK sports fans.
King Edward the Martyr is murdered at Corfe Castle, leading to the reign of Ethelred the Unready.
The birth of Wilfred Owen, the influential British poet of the First World War.
The birth of Madeline Carroll; the actress's work is widely available through UK streaming services.
The birth of David Livingstone, the Scottish explorer and missionary.
The Tolpuddle Martyrs are sentenced to transportation to Australia for forming a trade union.
The Academy Awards are televised for the first time, an event followed with interest in the UK.
Oliver Cromwell dissolves the Long Parliament.
The death of Sir Isaac Newton at the age of 84.
The birth of Fernando Torres; the striker's time in the Premier League made him a household name in the UK.
Thomas Cranmer, the first Protestant Archbishop of Canterbury, is burnt at the stake.
The Duke of Wellington fights a duel with the Earl of Winchilsea in London.
The birth of Ayrton Senna; his rivalry with British drivers is a key part of Formula One history.
The British Parliament votes to purchase 38 paintings to form the core of the National Gallery.
The birth of Andrew Lloyd Webber, the highly successful British composer of musical theatre.
A terrorist attack takes place at Westminster Bridge and the Houses of Parliament.
The birth of William Smith, the geologist who created the first nationwide geological map of Britain.
The death of Stendhal; the French writer's work is a staple of literary studies in the UK.
The birth of Sir Mo Farah, the quadruple Olympic gold medallist for Great Britain.
The death of Elizabeth I and the accession of James VI of Scotland to the English throne.
Robert Koch announces the discovery of the bacterium responsible for tuberculosis.
The Great Escape takes place from the Stalag Luft III prisoner of war camp.
Robert the Bruce is crowned King of Scotland at Scone Palace.
The Slave Trade Act is passed, abolishing the slave trade in the British Empire.
The birth of Elton John, the globally renowned British singer and pianist.
The birth of Thomas Dimsdale, the British physician who pioneered smallpox inoculation.
The death of Cecil Rhodes, the British imperialist and founder of Rhodesia.
The birth of Diana Ross; her music has been a fixture of the British charts for decades.
The death of King James I and the accession of Charles I to the throne.
The first ever rugby international is played between Scotland and England in Edinburgh.
The birth of James Callaghan, the only person to hold all four Great Offices of State in the UK.
The UK and France officially declare war on Russia, beginning the Crimean War.
The death of the influential British writer Virginia Woolf.
Lady Gaga is born; her work has had a significant impact on British pop music and fashion.
The Battle of Towton, the bloodiest battle ever fought on English soil, takes place.
The British North America Act is passed, creating the Dominion of Canada.
The UK officially triggers Article 50 to begin the process of leaving the European Union.
The Treaty of Paris is signed, bringing an end to the Crimean War.
The birth of Norah Jones; the artist's jazz-influenced pop is widely popular in the UK.
The death of Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother at the age of 101.
The death of Charlotte Brontë, the author of 'Jane Eyre'.
The Eiffel Tower is inaugurated in Paris; the feat of engineering is widely admired by British contemporaries.
The Poll Tax riots take place in London as thousands protest the new Community Charge.
